description: Geology Station 11 at North Ray Crater

tokens: Man, does this thing have steep walls.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Now, I tell you, I can't see to the bottom of it, and I'm just as close to the edge as I'm going to get.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Our plan here is to range along the edge of the crater for about 80 meters, if you can do that.
And, Charlie, if you'll start out with your pan 1, then the 500 millimeter.
We'd also like you to shoot some more pictures of Smoky with the 500, and then take your far field stereo.

And then if you range on out as far as you can go, taking the 500 millimeter with you, take - and John with you - take a stereo of the inside of the crater with the 500 millimeter from as far away from the Rover as you can get.
And then stick the 500 millimeter in John's SCB, and then do your other far-field polarimetry, and then, from then on, all we've got is sampling.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I think we probably ought to take all those things one at a time.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Do you want me to start out with the 500?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead and start out with your 500.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: You say, I start out with the 500?
Okay, Tony; I have magazine Kilo (with) frame count 1, I think, it was.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Those rocks you're looking at now, Tony, are white and they look breccious to me.
The big black one (meaning House Rock) is off behind the TV.
And you're going towards the rim on the crater right now (as Fendell pans clockwise).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The unfortunate thing about it, Houston, is that rascally rim...It goes down...It slopes into it about, say, 10 or 15 degrees, which is the kind of slope I'm standing on right now; and then, all of a sudden, in order to see to the bottom, I've got to walk another 100 yards down a 25- to 30-degree slope, and I don't think I'd better.
Maybe we can drive around to the other side and see down into it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Man, is that a hole in the ground!
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: The inside...(Stops to listen) It really is.
All I see is boulders around the crater.
There's nothing that reminds me of bedding, just loose boulders.
Though it might very well be that it's so shocked that there could be real boulders...I mean, real bedrock there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now, the layering, the boulder layers are horizontally oriented; and, of course, they are all covered with talus.
Over on the north wall, in particular, about 1/3 of the way from the top is a line of boulders that you probably ought to be able to see on the TV.
But they're all oriented right in that line, which would lead one to think that it has bedding there.
Don't you see that line right over there, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't...I'm worrying about trying to get this crazy (500-mm) camera going here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The material is just completely different...The wall...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...That line of boulders on the north wall, what color were they?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: In this light, they appear to be dark boulders.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Incidentally, the white rocks you see there.
Do they look like the Cone-Crater-type white rocks?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Better let me get a piece of one, Charlie.
I don't think...This is definitely a breccia right here, a big foot-and-a-half breccia.
It's a white matrix with dark clasts, and it looks to be a three-rock breccia.
Some of the dark clasts have even darker clasts than those.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Before leaving the Rover) I picked up (probably means "loaded into the camera") magazine Mike; it's on the 500.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, I just picked up a grab sample of a breccia.
It looks shocked; it has black clasts in it, black clasts (which are) a couple of millimeters across.
(Pause) It's so worn down that you know what it really looks like?
It looks like a...If I can use the analogy, (because) I'm not sure what the heck it is.
It just looks like a rock with a...You see, the clasts are sticking out of it, is what I'm saying.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What other pictures you want me to get with the 500?
I've done the interior of the crater.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like some more pictures of Smoky.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Get up here where I can see it (meaning Smoky Mountain).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...in your mineral description there, could you see a crystal shape?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: "Can I see a crystal shape?" I saw one clast.
(Pause) Well, the clasts in there are very angular.
Maybe that's a zap crater; that's probably what that is.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't see in the...The white matrix doesn't have any crystalline structure that I can recognize.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Passing on a question) And, Charlie, verify that you turned the DAC off.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Now, Tony, what is it you want me to do here?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What is it you want me to do here, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'd like Charlie, there, to go ahead and take his far-field pan of the crater and go on around and do a full pan.
It looks like you could probably do the thing from one place.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And, John, we'd like you to start ranging out in the best traverse direction for about 80 meters, if you can go that far.
And survey the area as you go out, and Charlie will follow you along, and then sample as you come back.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That'll be 80 meters to the northeast, here (toward House Rock).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (It's) no problem to do that, and...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Pan is complete; and I'm up to 165 on magazine Mike.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: My description of the crater: Sixty percent of it is covered with boulders up to 3 meters.
Make that 50 percent of it on the interior; we cannot see the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The boulders are splayed out from the center in rays that about every eighth to a quarter of a crater (circumference?)...you have a definite ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, John, did you get a bag number on that (grab sample)?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (To Tony) You still want me to take the 500 (when he and John go 80 meters to the northeast)?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Answering Tony's question about the sample bag number) Oh, yeah.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's in the bottom of SCB-7.
Anyway, I can identify that rock for you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Answering Charlie's question about taking the 500-mm lens) Yeah, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, do you still want me to get...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...after your 500 millimeter, do the far field pan and the three polarizer settings of the far side of the crater.
And then when you follow John along, take the 500 millimeter with you.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: You want me to help Tony to get - I mean Charlie - to get the rocks?
You want to get the near-field (photos) first, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're not doing a near field.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We're not going to do that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; John, I'm going to bring a sample bag (meaning an SCB) with that 500 millimeter in it, so we won't...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, I've got a sample bag, (meaning an SCB), here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm starting this polarimetry from about the 10 o'clock position of the Rover.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're starting in the Right position?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Pause) Yeah, I'm starting in the Right position.
I've got f/6, 1/25, at 74 (feet focal distance).
And I'll do a partial pan with each (filter) setting.
It's going to be about an eight-picture pan.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Man, I wish I could see the bottom of this beauty.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Did I drop my bags, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yeah, you dropped your bags; I thought you dropped them off the Rover.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That was going from right to left in the Right (filter) setting.
Center (filter) setting going from left to right.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Spotting the bag dispenser) There they are.
(Garbled; Pause) Should have told me I dropped them, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (The filter is) in the Left setting going from right to left.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, we can look out at my 12 o'clock position here, I can look down and see a large block that's on this inner flank here that I can't...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John, is there still something behind you?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's dust covered, and I can't tell you what type it is.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We thought we saw something still lying there...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Magazine Kilo; I'm up to 40 with the...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And I'm in the Left position; I'm going back to the right.
Now...Now, you want that stereo base, right?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like you to leave the polarizer on and take the 500 millimeter with you, also; and then range out your 80 meters.
You can either take a SCB...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: ...and stow the 500 millimeter (in it), or stick the 500 millimeter in John's SCB, when you're through with it.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; yours fell off...Where's yours that was on your back?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Oh, it fell off en route.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, if that thing fell off, the
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Probably get it on the way back, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: These things (meaning the SCBs) are giving us more trouble than the whole...(Pause) Okay, John, we'll save that one for rocks.
I'll put the camera in this one.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Have you got...I'm going to get the shovel.
(Pause) Tony, are you going to want a rake sample along the rim here?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's go get the polarizer first and...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: ...polarizing pictures yet, and we'll do the sampling on the way back.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: This is about as far as I'd like to go.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And I can't see the bottom of the crater though.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Pause) You see that big rock beyond John, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How about rolling that one over?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And when you get to a convenient time there, we'd like an EMU check.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; Tony, right under the upper dull-gray soil, there's a layer of a whitish material much like was in South Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll go over and...There's the shovel you can use to pick that up with, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; I'm going to get the far field (polarization pans) from right here, and I must have a 70- to 90-meter base, I'd say.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Maybe only 50...Let me move down a little bit farther.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, don't back into nothing around here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm going to pick up a sample which I think is the black-type rock; but it's sort of dust covered.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Starting the pan in the Right position, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Examining the sample) Well, I was wrong; it was a very friable - must be shocked - white rock with a lot of black clasts.
Fifty percent of the clasts in it are...or the clasts...or the matrix is...consist...(Chuckles) Try that (description) again.
Looks like about 50 percent of the rock is black clasts, which was a lot more than the last rock I picked up, and it sure is friable.
(Taking a sample bag off his camera) (That) probably means it's taken a heck of a beating.
And that's going into bag number 383.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Tony, the pan is complete - in all three settings - and I'm up to...(Long pause while he tries to read the frame counter)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you want a 500 from here also, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You probably have 20 or 25 pictures left in that 500, so maybe that'll give you the entire inside of the crater there.
(Pause) We'll just shoot up the rest of the roll in there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And if you're through with your far field, you can just throw that polarizer away.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Responding to Tony) I will in a minute.
Okay; you wanted an EMU check.
Flags are clear; I'm at 3.8 (psi) and about
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think about 75 percent, but it's so dusty I can't read it right now, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can't see mine either, Houston.
Can y'all see it down there?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm reading 3.85 (psi), I'm in Intermediate cooling.
When you get dust on the
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, you are all doing fine down here.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony; I'm doing some vertical stereos of these rays coming out of the crater.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How much of the inside did you get?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Oh, I got one partial pan of about three-quarters of the way up of the entire wall, and then almost two vertical rays.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I've got mine hooked over my little finger.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Look at this rock right here, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And it's really shocked, whatever it is.
It looks like chalk, Tony, it's so shocked.
It's about pebble size, and it's broken open.
Oh, let's make it 5 centimeters long, broken open.
John, could you bring me a...Let me get this one documented.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The polarizing filter's coming off (the LMP Hasselblad he is wearing).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we'll just call that the end of the 500 millimeter.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Sorry I ran out of film there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The black clasts in this rock are really (pause) black material.
It's either a very fine-grained black breccia...I'll tell you what it looks like.
It looks like that black breccia, fine-grained (garbled) that had that white clasts in it on Apollo 15.
Although here, the matrix is white, and the clasts are black.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Is this black breccia frothy, too, (like the Apollo 15 breccia)?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Answering Tony's question about clast size) Three centimeters.
(Answering Tony's second question) No, it's not frothy at all.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It could be a very dense, basalt-like rock.
It's cleavaged; I mean it looks like it has a 90-degree cleavage on it, and I'm hard put to tell that.
That's just the way it breaks.
It's too big to go in the bag, but I'm going to put it in there anyway.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: At least it has a shocked appearance.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What did you do with the shovel, Charlie!?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I thought you weren't using it.
I was just going to get this one over here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I finally got the polarizing filter off, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Looking back from where we are, Tony, towards the west - (correcting himself) south, rather - I can see South Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'd like to make sure we aren't overlooking something here, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's why I'd like to go on down to that black rock (meaning House Rock) down there, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You've really got your eye on, I can tell.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Here's a small secondary up here on top of the (North Ray) rim.
It's about a meter across, about a meter deep, and it has either very angular black glass or part of this black rock in total.
And they must be (pause) 4 or 5 centimeters across in there, and I'll get one or two of those babies.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Can I get a bag from you?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) You took my gnomon (meaning the scoop).
(To Tony) You'll have to get the shadow for scaling, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (To Tony, continuing his thought)...that white shocked rock.
It broke in two; there are two pieces of it.
(Getting the bag from John) (It's) partially documented - (in) the "befores" anyway - in 384.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And I'm going back (to the Rover) and get some (individual sample) bags.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now I've got Charlie's shovel for scale for the "before" shooting.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You can almost get local vertical on the shovel because you have to balance it before it will stand up.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie, did you bring the 500 millimeter back with you?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Hey, y'all must really be zoomed in (on John) then.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm just a few feet to the left of him (that is, just a few feet east of the line-of-sight from the TV to John).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Y'all didn't see any bags fall off anywhere, did you...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The outer surface of that rock is dust covered.
It appears to be a really black glass that's going into 385.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What a spectacular view looking back to the east and to the south, Tony.
Can see Baby Ray; way on past Kenesaw where there's a bright fresh crater down there on its flank.
I need to get some more bags, but I don't have a holder.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What really attracts me to this rock, even though it's dust covered, Houston, is the fact that it has right angles to it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Or, it did before I picked it up.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Remember those blocks up at Nevada Test Site?
They broke at right angles, too.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; this next one that's going in...It is so dust covered after I picked it up and dropped it into the dirt, I can't describe it to you; other than to say it's dust covered.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What do you want me to do now?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like big boulder samples, and look for that...Well, I guess, we're just boulder sampling now.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'd like to go up to the southwest around the rim in the other direction from John, and try my hand at these large white rocks.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, do you want to drive the (Rover toward House Rock?)...I don't think we can drive the Rover over to here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's not very much...I was just going up here a little bit, John, and do some flightline stereo of this 3-meter block up here.
(Pause) Tony, some of these rocks are glass covered.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's fine, Charlie, if you want to go down that way.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: If you're gonna boulder sample, Charlie, I'd better come and help you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, I'm just going to whack...I'm not really going to do the true thing.
I'll be down there to help you in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, let's...You want to go down to this big boulder down here?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'd like to, in a minute.
I wanted to make sure we get whatever this is up here on these white rocks.
I think you probably have it, but -
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John, how far away is that big boulder?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It is about, near as I can tell (pause), 150 meters, but the rocks around it are really something else.
That's the problem with trafficability up to it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If you think you can work up there, it sounds like an awful good place to work.
(Pause) Charlie, you just dropped your bags.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It didn't have a thing in it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; there's an old glassy rock, Tony, that - glass coated, anyway - that went into 415.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And it was hackley looking on the surface.
That's why I stopped to get it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, we could probably get a pretty good cross section up here with just a rake sample.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Like here is a little...a little crater right there that had uncovered some...(Pause) Okay, Tony.
I'm going to give you a little stereo on this boulder.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...or anything in it, a close-up might look good.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That's what I'm going to do.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, it is hot out here today, I'll tell you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Want to give me a hand with this rake sample, Charlie, or want me...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yeah, if you can stand by just a minute, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie described this boulder right here to you.
Did you describe this one with the black streak running through it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It has a black fracture pattern running right through the middle of it.
It's about six...It looks like a Sudbury breccia, and that's the truth.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Maybe we can get some of that.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And, Charlie, while you're up at that boulder, if you can, get...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: ...some of that fillet as well as the boulder.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; I don't have anything to (sample the) fillet with, but we'll see.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, I think with these equipment problems, we'd better work together, and I'll handle one bag, and you handle the other bag, and (we'll) be able to be more productive.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There's one of these white rocks up here (the westernmost), John, that's got a fracture on it, if you'll just let me...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The stuff is lying up there on the top.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Tony, we'll fillet for you - (fillet) sample for you up here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, I...No, I don't have a shovel, but I got a hammer.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I'm going to...I'll come up there and help you.
We can do the rake sampling...(Garbled) making (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie, if possible, we'd like to sample some of that stuff on top of the boulder.
I think that's what you're going to do there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: What I'm going to do...(Stops to listen) That's what I'm going to do.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're about ten steps ahead of us.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'm not going to give you any scale (in the picture), though; it's just too...With our problems here, like John said, it's...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you bring it back, that will be enough scale.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And it looks like it's the same thing that John has described.
It's a friable breccia with a black clast, being aphanitic.
The largest clast I see is, oh, not in this sample, but it's a black one that's a centimeter across.
It has a bluish tint to it, Tony.
It looks like all those shocked rocks that Fred Hörz was telling us about.
And that's in bag 4...Wait a minute.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And from today's experience and yesterday's, it sounds like old Fred's briefings were pretty useful.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: They turned out pretty good, I think.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, put that in my bag.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: See, both of...We'll just use this one.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: They want a fillet (sample) up here, John.
Could we get a fillet up there where that gnomon is?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Amused) I thought I'd use my little finger as a bag holder.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let me put it...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) There's an "after"; and I'll try to get a "locator" from up here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Did you get the boulder (sample) off the top?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The matrix is the white, though, Tony, with the black being the clasts.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I see at least two different colors of light-dark clasts.
Must be at least a three rocker.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You feel like you got all three (components)?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Let's get a rake sample, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Responding to Tony) I can't imagine how they wouldn't be in the clast that (pause) Charlie picked up.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Look at these rocks here that I just stepped on.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm chipping out this little teeny...this big black clast here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I don't think we got any in that sample I got.
(Pause) And the thing is so friable.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: While you're looking around up there, you might keep an eye out for a permanent shadow area and we'll go ahead and put it in a regular sample bag and forget the SESC.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: This black clast I chipped out is an aphanitic matrix with some...It looks like a typical (fine-grained) basalt to me.
And I'll show you...I can get a picture of it after I've chipped it out.
I didn't think I was going to be able to, but it came out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And that's a rock...(Stops to listen) Yeah.
I haven't seen a rock like that before in the Apollo samples.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I have a rock here that is a fine (grained), white crystalline rock.
It's pretty well dust covered, but I don't see any...I do not see any clasts in it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Of course, it could be just a hunk of matrix that got busted loose.
But as fine as these clasts are in it...That's going into bag 387.
As fine as these rocks are, I don't see how you can miss one.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (To Tony) Sorry we're working behind that big rock there, Tony, from the tube.
John, why don't we get out...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That way they can see us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, just anywhere we rake, we got a good sample.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's get a soil sample right here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I can get it with this.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yeah, we can; but I don't really see much...(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Boy, this equipment problem is really handicapping us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, the regolith here, Tony, up on this crater rim is really soft.
We're sinking in on these slopes about 6 inches or so.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The soil sample here is (in bag) 419.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's get in a clear spot, John, to rake.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, we can do it down there, too.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It looks almost fruitless up here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: One rake sample right out here, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can see us on the tube.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Oh, he's got some nice ones there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (If I can) see what I'm doing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And (they're) so dust covered, I can't really see what they are.
I can't believe all those bags dropped off.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Wait a minute; put that one in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Let me use the tongs and) save you some energy.
(Pause) Hey, here; give me my bag; I'll carry the bag.
(Taking his SCB from John) Got it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Tony...Why don't we go down halfway (back to the Rover), John, and do another rake sample; and then go down to the big, black rock?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And that'll be about 150-meter radial...(correcting himself) not radial, but concentric sampling.
(Pause) I'm on Intermediate cooling now, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: While you two are working together, you may be able to put the bag shoe into the core cap holder on the side of Charlie's PLSS.
You won't be able to run that way, but, at least, it'll be some place to hang it while you're working.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Here, John, let me...I'll take this (pair of tongs) down, and we'll get down in this little hollow.
If not, we'll use that for the gnomon.
How's that for the rake sample?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let me see if I can stick my bag into your holder like...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Won't be able to ride with it that way, huh?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, it'll probably pop out, but you can look at it.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'll just keep it on my...[Stops to listen]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Keep it over your...Push it way up on your finger.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If I push it up on my middle finger, it ain't going to fall off.
I won't even know it's there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm going to get a down-Sun.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John, have I still got my SCB on my back?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John's getting about two rakes; he's done two rakes.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's a pretty good, little sample.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, one of them had...I could see vesicles in one of them.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And we'd like a soil (sample) here.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: My shopping bag idea would have worked, John.
Those things are sitting right up; you just plop them down.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sure, sure it would have worked, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, (sample bag) 422 for the soil sample, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, get an "after" of that, please, while I pick this bag up.
transcript.speaker: Duke
